dailyJudgementSnippet: Google Gemini won on sheer front-page presence: two Gemini 3.1 Pro threads generated sustained discussion, and the overall tone stayed mildly favorable because commenters highlighted price, longer-context improvements, and some impressive outputs even while calling out reliability and tool-use problems [E1][E3][E5].
winnerExplanation: Google Gemini had the strongest combination of coverage and usable praise. Hacker News spent the day directly testing and comparing Gemini 3.1 Pro, and the positive notes on cost efficiency, context retention, and occasional coding wins were substantial enough to outweigh recurring complaints about loops, odd edits, and multilingual weirdness [E1][E3][E5].

openai.judgementSnippet: OpenAI picked up credit for permissive third-party tool support and some favorable Codex-versus-Opus anecdotes, while comments in the exoskeleton thread pushed back on leadership talk about sharply reducing engineering jobs [E9][E6].

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Claude and Opus got favorable head-to-head mentions in Gemini discussions, but the day was pulled down by backlash to Anthropic's third-party auth ban, skepticism toward its autonomy metrics, and criticism of replacement-heavy executive messaging [E2][E8][E11][E7].

google_gemini.judgementSnippet: Two front-page Gemini 3.1 Pro posts kept attention high; commenters credited price, longer context, and occasional standout outputs, but also complained about loops, tool use, and an unsettling Arabic persona example [E1][E3][E5].

microsoft_copilot.judgementSnippet: Microsoft Copilot's signal came almost entirely from being cited as the more developer-friendly side of the third-party access debate, especially around OpenCode-style usage [E10].

evidence.summary: Gemini 3.1 Pro drew heavy attention, with commenters split between frustration over loops and tool use and praise for lower pricing and some strong reasoning or coding moments.
In the Gemini launch thread, several commenters treated Claude and Opus as the practical coding standard Gemini still failed to match, giving Anthropic favorable comparison lift.
The second Gemini 3.1 Pro front-page thread still had commenters praising longer-context retention and more complex outputs, even while noting remaining shortcuts on constrained coding tasks.
A commenter described Gemini's Arabic responses as oddly religious and potentially biasing, which hurt confidence in its multilingual behavior.
The 'AI is not a coworker' thread included backlash to OpenAI leadership rhetoric about sharply reducing demand for software engineers.
That same thread also targeted Anthropic leadership and Claude Code messaging as evidence of a replacement-minded strategy.
Anthropic's ban on using subscription auth in third-party tools was framed in comments as hostile lock-in and value capture.
In contrast, commenters praised OpenAI's more permissive stance toward third-party developer tools and some reported Codex outperforming Opus.
GitHub and Microsoft Copilot were cited as allowing OpenCode-style access, which commenters treated as the more developer-friendly side of the policy split.
Comments on Anthropic's agent-autonomy research called the 99.9th-percentile framing cherry-picked and disingenuous.
